title: Sweet and Sour Chicken

ingredients.name: boneless chicken (breast or thigh), cut into bite-size pieces

subs.name: paneer cubes or firm tofu
subs.notes: batter and fry the same way; tofu may need more careful handling

ingredients.name: cornflour
visual_checks: 3 tbsp for batter, 1 tbsp for sauce slurry
ingredients.notes: 3 tbsp for batter, 1 tbsp for sauce slurry

subs.name: 2 tbsp tomato paste + 1 tbsp extra sugar
subs.notes: ketchup is preferred as it brings sweetness and flavour in one

subs.name: 2 tbsp lemon juice
subs.notes: reduces frying temperature to avoid splatter

ingredients.name: capsicum (any colour), cut into 3 cm squares

subs.name: 1 diced mango or skip entirely
subs.notes: raw mango works beautifully for tartness

ingredients.name: neutral cooking oil, for deep frying and stir-frying
visual_checks: 500 ml for frying; you will only consume a fraction of this
ingredients.notes: 500 ml for frying; you will only consume a fraction of this

steps.text: Season the chicken pieces with salt. Mix the beaten egg and 3 tablespoons of cornflour with just enough water (about 2–3 tablespoons) to make a thick batter. Coat the chicken evenly in the batter.
Mix the sweet-sour sauce in a bowl: ketchup, vinegar, sugar, soy sauce, the remaining 1 tablespoon of cornflour and 100 ml water. Stir until the sugar dissolves and set aside.

Heat the oil in a kadai or wok to 170°C (a cube of bread should sizzle and turn golden in about 40 seconds). Working in two batches, slide the battered chicken in and fry for 4–5 minutes until golden and cooked through. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on paper.
While the chicken rests, pour off most of the frying oil, leaving about 1 tablespoon in the wok. Fry the garlic for 30 seconds on medium-high heat until golden.
Add the capsicum and stir-fry for 2 minutes until just softened but still vibrant. Add the pineapple if using and toss for 30 seconds.
Give the sauce a quick stir and pour it into the wok. Bring to a bubble over medium heat, stirring, until the sauce thickens into a glossy glaze, about 1–2 minutes.
Slide the fried chicken back in and toss quickly to coat — work fast so the batter stays crispy. Serve at once over steamed rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → paneer cubes or firm tofu (batter and fry the same way; tofu may need more careful handling); pineapple → 1 diced mango or skip entirely (raw mango works beautifully for tartness); vinegar → 2 tbsp lemon juice (reduces frying temperature to avoid splatter); tomato-ketchup → 2 tbsp tomato paste + 1 tbsp extra sugar (ketchup is preferred as it brings sweetness and flavour in one)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: All ingredients are easily found in India — use Kissan or Maggi ketchup, Chings soy sauce and plain white vinegar. Shimla mirch (capsicum) and canned pineapple are available at most supermarkets. Deep-fry the chicken in your kadai over medium-high heat; maintain the oil temperature by frying in small batches. Ananas (pineapple) is an excellent substitute if canned fruit is unavailable.
